commands.repair_reason: Needs contributor/maintainer follow-up for the authDir guard and real WhatsApp after-fix proof; automation should not repair this PR while contributor environment proof is missing.; Needs attention: The diff has a concrete auth-boundary concern because it can route an old captured WhatsApp reply through a successor socket without verifying the same auth state.; Review finding: [P1] Guard successor sockets by auth di... (sha=dc98e608b3ea73f19e0840566714e117652d9860)
commands.review_summary:
The PR exposes a WhatsApp controller `getCurrentSock()` handle and makes inbound reply/read/group metadata paths fall back to the registered successor controller socket when the original monitor socket ref is null, with a regression test.

Reproducibility: yes. via source inspection, though not by a live run in this read-only review. Shutdown clears the controller socket ref and stops retries, while the current send path throws when `getCurrentSock()` returns null; the PR body also includes redacted production logs for the same 408 restart pattern.
commands.review_followup: Needs contributor/maintainer follow-up for the authDir guard and real WhatsApp after-fix proof; automation should not repair this PR while contributor environment proof is missing.

target.title: fix(whatsapp): route captured replies through successor controller after restart
target.body:
## Summary

When the gateway's `channel-health-monitor` tears down a WhatsApp `ConnectionController` mid-run, any in-flight inbound message's captured `reply()` closure is left bound to the now-stale `socketRef` of the shutdown controller. The retry loop in `sendTrackedMessage` only knows how to wait for that same controller's `socketRef` to be repopulated, but the successor controller constructs its own fresh `socketRef` object — so the captured reply throws `RECONNECT_IN_PROGRESS_ERROR` indefinitely.

**Visible symptom:** the agent silently fails to reply to a WhatsApp message after a transient 408 disconnect that triggered a health-monitor restart. The user's message shows "read", then nothing.

## Forensic reproducer

Observed in production 2026-05-22:

```
12:00:39.242  [whatsapp] inbound message in group <redacted>@g.us
12:02:27.820  [whatsapp] Web connection closed (status 408 Request Time-out). Retry 1/12 in 2.14s…
12:02:27.934  [health-monitor] [whatsapp:default] health-monitor: restarting (reason: disconnected)
                    ↑ controller A shutdown ~114ms after the 408 close
12:02:34.182  → 12:02:35.650  controller B boots on a fresh socket
12:04:44.419  auto-reply delivery failed (correlationId=<redacted>)
12:04:44.421  auto-reply delivery failed (correlationId=<redacted>)
                    ↑ A's captured reply finally times out: no active socket - reconnection in progress
```

`sendTrackedMessage` reads `socketRef.current` via `getCurrentSock()` — that's A's `socketRef`, permanently null after A's shutdown. The `connection-controller-registry` already tracks the live successor (B), but the handle only exposes `getActiveListener()` — no socket access.

## Fix

* Extend `WhatsAppConnectionControllerHandle` at `extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller-registry.ts` with `getCurrentSock(): WASocket | null`.
* `WhatsAppConnectionController` implements it by returning its own `socketRef.current`.
* `attachWebInboxToSocket`'s `getCurrentSock()` helper falls back to `getRegisteredWhatsAppConnectionController(accountId)?.getCurrentSock()` when the local `socketRef.current` is null.

The fallback fires only when the local socket is gone, so the steady-state hot path is unchanged. All current `getCurrentSock()` callers — `sendTrackedMessage`, `groupMetadata`, `readMessages` — benefit, so post-restart group mentions and read receipts also recover, not just plain text sends.

## Trade-off worth maintainer eyes

The fallback only swaps the socket object, not the listener's auth state (`authDir`, captured `lidLookup`, etc.). For the typical health-monitor restart — same `accountId`, same `authDir`, fresh socket — this is correct: the new socket's LID store + the original `authDir` are consistent for the same paired account.

For a hypothetical relink scenario where the same `accountId` is re-paired to a different `authDir` between restarts, the captured `reply()` would send through the new socket but with JID resolution that still uses the original `authDir`. Codex review flagged this, and I think it's a real concern only for that relink edge case. Happy to add an `authDir`-match guard on the handle (e.g. extend the handle with `getAuthDir(): string` and gate the fallback on equality) if maintainers want belt-and-suspenders.

## Test plan

- [x] New Vitest at `extensions/whatsapp/src/monitor-inbox.streams-inbound-messages.test-support.ts` — full lifecycle: controller A handles inbound, A shutdown (socketRef null + retries aborted + unregistered), B registers with own live socket, captured `A.reply()` routes through B's socket
- [x] `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/monitor-inbox.behavior.test.ts` — 62/62 passed
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt --check`
- [x] `pnpm lint:extensions`
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — full lane green (typecheck, lint, import cycles, deps guards)
- [x] `codex review --base upstream/main` — 2 iterations addressed (group-meta/read-receipt fallback fixed by lifting the fallback into `getCurrentSock`; auth-state edge case documented above)

## Note on test isolation

3 tests in `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound.media.test.ts` are red on this branch but are also red on clean `upstream/main` (`fcb9c46af0`) with no diff applied — they fail with `Error: WhatsApp runtime not initialized` at `durable-receive.ts:59`, a setup issue unrelated to this PR's surface. Verified by `git stash`.

## AI disclosure

Authored with Claude Code (Opus 4.7) assistance. Fully tested. Author confirms understanding of the change.
